description: Address review findings: (1) Add defaultScope to User model excluding password, use User.unscoped() in login. (2) Use Sequelize.literal with array_cat/array_remove for atomic bookedSeats operations in booking.service.js (with SQLite fallback).
steps: Add defaultScope to User model that excludes the password field from queries by default.
Update auth.controller.js login to use User.unscoped().findOne() so password is available for comparison.
Update booking.service.js confirmBooking to use Sequelize.literal with array_cat for PostgreSQL, with SQLite fallback using read-modify-write.
Update booking.service.js cancelBooking to use Sequelize.literal with array_remove for PostgreSQL, with SQLite fallback.
acceptance_criteria: User.findByPk() and User.findOne() no longer return password field by default
Login still works (uses unscoped query to get password for comparison)
confirmBooking uses SQL-level array_cat on PostgreSQL for atomic append
cancelBooking uses SQL-level array_remove on PostgreSQL for atomic removal
SQLite fallback still works for tests (read-modify-write pattern)
findings: Both review fixes implemented and verified. All 61 integration tests pass with SQLite. The defaultScope on User excludes password from all default queries - auth middleware (findByPk) no longer leaks password hash onto req.user. Login uses User.unscoped().findOne() to retrieve password for comparison. Atomic array_cat/array_remove via Sequelize.literal is used for PostgreSQL dialect, with SQLite fallback using the existing read-modify-write pattern (safe because SQLite serializes all writes anyway).
